text.tspan: On a MISS, the FIRST request acquires a 'compute in progress' lock for that key.
Subsequent requests for the same key WAIT for the first to finish, then read its result.
Only ONE DB query happens regardless of concurrency.
LIBRARIES:
u2022 Go: golang.org/x/sync/singleflight
u2022 Java: Caffeine's CacheLoader (built in)
u2022 Python: aiocache, request_coalescer
u2022 Ruby: identity_cache, dalli with race_condition_ttl
This alone solves 80% of stampedes.
Each read of a value near its TTL has a small (and growing) probability
of being chosen to refresh EARLY, before actual expiry.
Distributes refreshes across time u2192 no synchronized expiry stampede.
Formula (Vattani et al. 2015 'Optimal Probabilistic Cache Stampede Prevention'):
if now - (delta u00d7 beta u00d7 ln(random())) > expiry, refresh now
No locks needed; statistically smooth.
Identify hot keys; refresh them on a schedule BEFORE they expire.
App reads always hit a populated cache. Background worker handles all DB load.
WORKS FOR: a small set of well-known hot keys.
DOESN'T HELP: long-tail or unpredictable hot keys.
Keep an OLD value past its expiry. On MISS:
u2022 first request triggers async refresh
u2022 all requests (including the trigger) get the STALE value immediately
u2022 once refresh completes, future reads get fresh
HTTP standard: stale-while-revalidate header (Vercel CDN, Cloudflare, browsers).
Varnish: 'grace mode'. CDN folks have always done this.
Don't use TTL = exactly 300s for everything. Use 300 + random(0, 30)s.
Deployed at the same time? Cache entries still expire at slightly different times.
Prevents 'fleet-wide synchronized expiry'.
Easy, cheap, prevents one whole class of stampedes.
